title: Fix /about-us/ city page namespace contamination
description:
City service pages are nested under /about-us/ which tells Google these are company information pages, not local service pages. 

Affected URLs: 
  • /about-us/granite-bay-ca/
  • /about-us/sacramento-ca/hvac/
  • /about-us/sacramento-ca/electrician/
  • /about-us/rocklin-ca/hvac/
  • /about-us/rocklin-ca/electrician/
  • /about-us/citrus-heights-ca/hvac/
  • /about-us/fair-oaks-ca/
  • /about-us/carmichael-ca/
  • All sub-service variants under these

Actions:
  1. Map each to its correct /city-ca/ equivalent
  2. 301 redirect all /about-us/city/ pages to the correct /city-ca/ URL
  3. Remove from sitemap
  4. Update all internal links

title: Fix trailing slash canonical inconsistency
description:
15 pages appear in the GSC 'Alternate page with proper canonical' report because trailing slash variants are being crawled as separate URLs (e.g. /roseville-ca/ vs /roseville-ca//). 

Action: Add a site-wide redirect rule in .htaccess or the WordPress redirect plugin normalising all URLs to the trailing-slash version. Fixes all 15 pages in one change. 

Data: GSC — Alternate page with proper canonical tag export (15 rows) 
https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Hcwlja659XGeF2O6aBuO4qPj7-cVOIUbYlC5R2_bWSs/edit?usp=sharing

title: Consolidate generator pages to 1 canonical
description:
14 generator-related pages exist across 3 namespaces. The generator sizing page has 10,843 monthly impressions but authority is fragmented. 

All URLs to consolidate: /generator-services/, /generators, /electrical/generators/, /electrical/generator-faqs/, /generators/transfer-switches/, /generators/transfer-switches/transfer-switch-installation/, /transfer-switches/, /transfer-switch-installation/, /generator-transfer-switch-installation/, /portable-generators/, /whole-house-generators-lincoln-ca/, /portable-generators-lincoln-ca/ 

Actions: 
  1. Designate /electrical/generators/ as the single canonical generator hub
  2. 301 redirect all other generator URLs to /electrical/generators/ (except transfer switches)
  3. Designate /electrical/transfer-switch-installation/ as canonical for transfer switch content
  4. 301 redirect /transfer-switches/, /generators/transfer-switches/, /generator-transfer-switch-installation/ to it
  5. Deploy FAQPage schema on the canonical generator hub

title: Consolidate EV charger, surge protection, smart home and residential electrical duplicates
description:
EV Charger (3 → 1): Keep /electrical-ev-charger-installation/. Redirect /ev-charger-installation/ and /electrical/ev-charging/ to it. 

Surge Protection (2 → 1): Keep /electrical/electrical-whole-house-surge-protection/. Redirect /whole-house-surge-protection/ to it. 

Smart Home (2 → 1): Keep /electrical-smart-home-services/. Redirect /smart-home-services/ to it. 

Residential Electrical (3 → 1): Keep /electrical-residential/. Redirect /residential-electrical-services/ and /roseville-electric-services/ to it.

title: Implement clean silo structure, update navigation and internal links
description:
Once duplicates are consolidated, update navigation and internal linking to match the clean structure: 

ELECTRICAL → /electrical/ (parent) with 14 sub-pages all under /electrical/service-name/ COOLING → /cooling/ (parent) with 5 sub-pages HEATING → /heating/ (parent) with 7 sub-pages WATER HEATERS → /water-heaters/ (parent) with 4 sub-pages LOCATIONS → /locations/ (new hub) with all city pages underneath 

Navigation change: 

  • Remove: Electric / Cooling / Heating / Water Heaters / About Us / Financing
  • Replace with: Services ▼ / Locations ▼ / Wingman Club / About Us ▼ / [Book Appointment]
Internal linking rule going forward: every sub-page links back to its silo parent. Every silo parent links down to all sub-pages.

,

Need your assistance with reaching out to the client to have them cancel their Yext subscription effective immediately versus at the end of the billing cycle, so we can create and add them under our account.

This need derives from the current GBP name change, as we have systems in place before making important GBP changes, to help combat potential reverifications and suspensions from said changes, as well as ensuring NAPW consistency across all digital assets.

Here is the guide with all of the information you will need to do this, but let me know if you need my help. Feel free to also CC me on any emails if you'd like, and I can jump in to assist if needs be brother.

Yext - Cancel Active Services for a Yext Listing - CLIXSY HQ

IMPORTANT NOTES:
- Yext does not allow a third party to communicate on behalf of a client. The client/account owner has to handle all communication Yext.
- Cancelling active services with Yext is required before we can add them to our account. This process only cancels services — it does not delete the listing or account.

title: Include Sitemap In footer of Client websites
description:
Hi, this is inline with previous task - Include Sitemap In footer of Client websites - J389 Go Arco

Here is a sheet we can also use for reference: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1qPfUUC1J2r1s7Ot8Y1PmIcje6WpyMszDhxHGz4TxDTk/edit?gid=0#gid=0

SEO added more info column D. 

I have included the task info here:
--- 
Hey Team!

To enhance our technical SEO baseline, improve crawl efficiency, and ensure consistent signaling across all client properties, It would be great to come to an agreement to outlining a new, standardized protocol for crawl management.

My suggestion is as follows:

Implement the following three directives on all client websites as part of our standard build and maintenance process.

1. XML Sitemap in robots.txt (Primary Directive)

This is the most effective way to inform search engines of all indexable URLs.

Action: Ensure the robots.txt file for every client domain contains a Sitemap directive pointing to the full URL of the XML sitemap.

Implementation Example:
User-agent: 
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap.xml

The "Why": This provides a clear, direct path for Google to find and crawl our most important pages, ensuring faster discovery and indexing of new content.

2. User-Facing HTML Sitemap (Footer)

This is for user experience and secondary crawl pathing.

Action: All client websites must have a user-facing HTML sitemap.

Implementation: This sitemap must be linked in the footer of every page on the site.

The "Why": This provides a clear navigation path for users, improves site accessibility, and   acts as a secondary crawl path for search engines to discover deep pages.

Strategic Crawl & Indexation Control

This is critical. We must be precise in telling crawlers what to crawl and what to index.

A. To Control Crawling (Using robots.txt)

We must Disallow crawlers from accessing low-value, non-SEO pages to conserve crawl budget.

Action: Audit and update the robots.txt file to Disallow non-public or utility sections of the site.
Implementation Examples:
Disallow: /wp-admin/

The WP sitemap page  plugin in perfect to use as a plugin for now

Regarding providing a robot.txt:

They differ per client especially regarding disallows but here is a comprehensive one that can be edited according to client needs especially regarding disallows:

User-agent: *

# --- Core WordPress File Disallows ---
# Block all core administrative and include folders.
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Disallow: /wp-includes/

# --- Crawl Budget Optimization ---
# Block non-media plugin and theme files from being crawled.
Disallow: /wp-content/plugins/
Disallow: /wp-content/themes/

# Block internal search result pages, which are low-quality, duplicate content.
Disallow: /?s=
Disallow: /search/

# Block other non-public WordPress files and pages.
Disallow: /wp-login.php
Disallow: /readme.html
Disallow: /trackback/
Disallow: /xmlrpc.php

# --- Allow Directives for Critical Files ---
# Explicitly ALLOW critical AJAX and asset files that Google
# may need for rendering, even if their parent folder is disallowed.
Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php
Allow: /wp-content/uploads/

# --- Sitemap Directive ---
# This is the primary directive to tell all search engines where our XML sitemap is located.
# [IMPORTANT: Replace with the client's actual sitemap URL]
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap_index.xml

Also, just an example of placement. Sitemap listed at the very bottom of the footer on the left followed by privacy policy etc.

* In the footer we will use the page /sitemap/ and not the XML sitemap.  The XML sitemap is for Google & the robots.txt.

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1oNmqXYRsgamzmaHE8-H0RKw3q1xBznuu-TZR_F035bs/edit?usp=sharing

8. Conclusion (first so you can see it immediately)


The foundation of Maverick Electrical’s website provides a rare advantage: clean URLs, clear service silos, and existing top-level pages. By building out robust pillar pages, linking them strategically to semantically optimized cluster pages, and applying technical best practices, the business can position itself as a leading authority in the region for all core service areas. 

 

6. Action Plan 


Step 1: Pillar Page Review and Enhancement 
  • Review and expand existing pillar pages to ensure entity coverage (for example, regulations, energy efficiency, cost considerations).
  • Confirm heading structures are consistent and semantically aligned.
Step 2: Build Out Cluster Pages
  • Expand clusters for each silo to cover 5–10 high-value subtopics.
  • Ensure cluster pages link back to their pillar page with keyword-rich anchors.
Step 3: Internal Linking Network
  • Add cross-silo links where logical (for example, Electrical + Heating for smart thermostat installation).
  • Ensure links are natural and semantically descriptive.
Step 4: Schema and Technical Optimization
  • Add Service, LocalBusiness, and FAQ schema.
  • Optimize image ALT text for semantic relevance.
  • Improve metadata with descriptive titles and rich snippets.
Step 5: Localized Expansion
  • Build city-specific service pages (for example, “Plumbing in Sacramento” or “Electrical Services in Roseville”).
  • Use localized testimonials and case studies to support these pages.

7. Expected Outcomes


By implementing this structured pillar and cluster strategy, Maverick Electrical can expect: 
  • Improved topical authority in Plumbing, Electrical, Heating, and Cooling.
  • Higher organic visibility for both core and long-tail keywords.
  • Stronger local SEO signals for service areas across Northern California.
  • Enhanced user experience through logical navigation and well-structured content hubs.

title: Task for Mavericks Electrical – Service Page Heading Structure Update
description:
Issue:
All current Mavericks Electrical service pages are missing an <h1> heading. The top-level heading on each page is currently set as an <h2>, which is incorrect for semantic structure and negatively impacts accessibility, SEO, and content hierarchy.


Task:
Update all service pages to ensure that each page includes a single, clearly defined <h1> heading at the top of the page.


Requirements:

  • Each page must have one <h1> as the primary title.

  • The existing <h2> heading should be elevated to an <h1> where appropriate.

  • Ensure no duplicate <h1> tags on the same page.

  • Maintain proper heading hierarchy: <h2> for subsections, <h3> for nested content, etc.


Goal:
Align all service pages with HTML5 semantic standards to improve crawlability, user experience, and SEO performance.
